coredata.dc:title: The bright fluorescence of non-aromatic molecules in aqueous solution originates from pH-induced CTE behavior
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunqian
Zhang, Hao
Wang, Kexin
Cao, Duxia
Wang, Kaiming
Guan, Ruifang
Zhou, Chuanjian
coredata.dc:description: Non-aromatic fluorescent materials with inherent visible light emission have received widespread attention. In this work, a biomimetic fluorescent molecule CA-AEP with a dipeptide structure is introduced. CA-AEP will emit bright biomimetic fluorescence in aqueous solutions by adjusting the pH, which has never been reported. This unique luminescent characteristic can be rationalized by the clustering-triggered emission (CTE) mechanism. In addition, CA-AEP can be used to monitor the maximum dynamic pH in the alkaline range of aqueous systems. Finally, the cytotoxicity assay to A549 cells showed that CA-AEP was non-toxic. Therefore, this work provides a new type of luminogen, which has potential application prospects in the field of environmental monitoring and cell biology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-aromatic fluorescent materials
Intrinsic fluorescence emission
Clustering-triggered emission
Biological cell imaging
Luminescence mechanism
Extremely alkaline pH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectra of CA-AEP . (b) Excitation-dependent emission properties of CA-AEP with an aqueous solution concentration of 30 mg mL−1 at different excitation wavelengths (Inset: normalized emission). (c) The fluorescence decay of CA-AEP (laser wavelength: 375 nm, χ2 = 1.189). (d) Photographs of solid CA-AEP under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Photographs of CA-AEP aqueous solutions (0.1 M) at different pH (2.0–13.0) under 365 nm UV light. (b) Fluorescence intensity of CA-AEP aqueous solution with pH in the range of 2.0–13.0. (c) Zeta potentials of CA-AEP aqueous solution at different pH values (2.0, 7.0, 10.0, 12.0, 13.0). (d) UV–vis absorption spectra under different pH values (7.0–13.0). (f) Excitation and emission under different pH values (7.0–13.0). (e) UV–vis absorption (447 nm) and (g) fluorescence (440 nm) intensity as a function of pH based on pH titration (7.0–13.0).
Gradual ionization process and pI morphological transformations of CA-AEP .

(a) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of CA-AEP 12 and CA-AEP 12-7.4. (b) Fluorescence intensity of CA-AEP solution at different pH (The pH of the CA-AEP solution was adjusted from 12.0 to 7.4 and then to 12.0 for three cycles).
(a) Cytotoxicity of CA-AEP 12-7.4 on A549 cells determined by MTT. (b) Fluorescent images of A549 cells (A1, A2, A3), A549 cells incubated with CA-AEP 12-7.4 (50 μM; B1, B2, B3).
The synthesis process (a) and the response pH process (b) of CA-AEP .

head.ce:title: The bright fluorescence of non-aromatic molecules in aqueous solution originates from pH-induced CTE behavior

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological Science and Technology, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological Science and Technology, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yunqian Ma and Hao Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Non-aromatic fluorescent materials with inherent visible light emission have received widespread attention. In this work, a biomimetic fluorescent molecule CA-AEP with a dipeptide structure is introduced. CA-AEP will emit bright biomimetic fluorescence in aqueous solutions by adjusting the pH, which has never been reported. This unique luminescent characteristic can be rationalized by the clustering-triggered emission (CTE) mechanism. In addition, CA-AEP can be used to monitor the maximum dynamic pH in the alkaline range of aqueous systems. Finally, the cytotoxicity assay to A549 cells showed that CA-AEP was non-toxic. Therefore, this work provides a new type of luminogen, which has potential application prospects in the field of environmental monitoring and cell biology.

ce:section.ce:para: Nonaromatic luminescent materials have received increasing attention due to their excellent water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, excellent biodegradability, low cytotoxicity and inherent fluorescence emission properties, such as poly(amino esters)s [1], poly(amidoamines) [2], polyethyleneimine [3], hyperbranched poly(amido amine) [4–6], poly(ether amide)s [7] and other dendritic and hyperbranched polymers bearing aliphatic tertiary amine and amide moieties. Generally, the N-branched tertiary amines of such polymers are considered to be the fluorescent centres [1,2,8]. Recently, the luminescence of non-aromatic natural products has attracted people's attention, especially non-aromatic amino acids related to the background fluorescence of living organisms [9–12]. Homchaudhuri et al. [13] found that the absorption peak at 270 nm and the blue emission peak at 435 nm can be clearly detected when the concentration of the L-lysine aqueous solution was as high as 0.5 M, and the fluorescence emission was attributed to the aggregation of L-lysine. Yuan et al. [9] found that most non-aromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids) have intrinsic visible emission in concentrated solutions and solid powders. They attributed the emission of non-aromatic amino acids to the clustering-triggered emission (CTE) mechanism [14–16]. In other words, the clustering of nontraditional chromophores causes the electron clouds to overlap with simultaneous conformation rigidification. Ravanfar et al. [11] found that non-aromatic amino acids had almost no emission in the solution, but showed a wide range of fluorescence emission in the crystal state. They believe that the luminescence of crystalline non-aromatic amino acids originates from the tight network structure induced by hydrogen bonding. Although the mechanism of fluorescence is still under debate, it is clear that it is difficult for non-aromatic luminescent materials to emit strong fluorescence in solutions, whether it is high molecular polymers or small molecules.

In this work, we report a new type of non-aromatic emitter CA-AEP with a dipeptide structure (Scheme 1a). CA-AEP has excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. Under extremely alkaline conditions, CA-AEP can turn on bright blue fluorescence in aqueous solutions. In addition, CA-AEP also shows the potential to accurately monitor the maximum dynamic pH of aqueous systems. Finally, CA-AEP has low cytotoxicity and is used for biological cell imaging.

Citric acid, anhydrous ethanol, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, n-hexane, tetrahydrofuran, thionyl chloride and N-aminoethylpiperazine (AEP) were purchased from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. 1H NMR (400 MHz) and 13C NMR (100 MHz) spectra were recorded in D2O on a MercuryPlus-400 spectrometer. Fluorescence decay curve was attained on a time-correlated single photon-counting spectrofluorometer system (Fluorocube-01, JY-IBH, Horiba). Fluorescence QY were obtained on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer with an integrating sphere assembly. The following instruments were also used for different tests involved: dynamic light scattering nanosizer (DLS, Nano-ZS 3600, Malvern), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, QUANTA FEG 250), high resolution mass spectra (HRMS, Agilent Q-TOF6510), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100plus).

According to Refs. [17,18], 5 mmol (0.97 g) citric acid was sufficiently dissolved in 20 ml tetrahydrofuran. 20 mmol (2.38 g) thionyl chloride was slowly added to the above solution under stirring in an ice bath, and the mixed solution was stirred to continue the reaction for 12 h. Then, the reaction product citric acid chloride (CA-Cl) of the first step was purified through evaporating to remove excess unreacted thionyl chloride (Figure S1). Next, all intermediates were dissolved in 40 ml tetrahydrofuran, and then 6 mmol (0.78 g) AEP (nCA:nAEP = 1:1.2) was slowly added under stirring in an ice bath. After stirring for 3 h, CA-AEP was purified by anhydrous ethanol, and the obtained white solid was dried in rotary evaporator at 0 °C. The yield was 85.7%.

The CA-AEP was dissolved in deionized water at a concentration of 1.0 mM and placed in an environment of 4 °C for use. In the pH titration experiment, HCl and NaOH were used to adjust the pH of the solution. All fluorescence detection experiments were performed at room temperature.

The cytotoxicity of CA-AEP 12-7.4 was detected by MTT method [19]. In short, when A549 cells were seeded in 96-well plates, the concentration of CA-AEP 12-7.4 was 0 (blank test), 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50 μM. The same method was used to test the cytotoxicity of CA-AEP 12-7.4. The prepared CA-AEP 12-7.4 aqueous solution (50 μM) was added to A549 cell culture medium and incubated at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 2 h. After the cultivation and cleaning, the fluorescence imaging and cell structure were examined under laser scanning confocal fluorescence microscopy.

The structures of the synthesized CA-AEP were characterized by 1H NMR, and 13C NMR and HRMS. Figure S2 shows the 1H NMR spectrum of CA-AEP . In Figure S2, the signals at 3.12, 3.06, 2.77, 2.68, 2.59–2.46, and 2.45–2.30 ppm come from protons of methylene groups of CH 2-NH-CH 2, CONH-CH 2-CH2-N, CONH-CH2-CH 2-N, N-CH 2-CH2-NH, COOH-CH 2, C-CH 2-CONH, severally. In addition, the proton signals of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups cannot be detected because D2O was used as the solvent of CA-AEP . Figure S3 is the 13C NMR spectrum of CA-AEP , the signals of secondary carbons at 53.75, 49.03, 45.05, 45.01, 43.80 and 36.24 ppm corresponded to C3, C2, C6, C9, C1 and C4, respectively. The signals at 179.60 and 176.52 ppm are attributed to carboxylic carbon (COOH) of C8 and C10, separately, the signal at 170.84 ppm is ascribed to amido carbon (CONH-) of C5, and the signal at 75.82 ppm is ascribed to quaternary carbon of C7. The presence of the signal of COOH and the results of HRMS (Figure S4) indicated that the remaining two COOH of citric acid cannot be consumed by the excessive amino of AEP, which may be due to the increasing stereohindrance effect.

The optical properties of CA-AEP in aqueous solution were studied using UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. Two obvious absorption bands can be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fig. 1 a. The weaker absorption band at 265–300 nm is related to the n-σ* electronic transition of isolated carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups [20]. The absorption band at 315–420 nm can be attributed to the n-π* electron transition of carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl clusters [21–23]. Namely, the lone pair electrons of nitrogen and oxygen atoms overlap in the clusters to produce the spatial electron delocalization system [9,14]. In addition, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of CA-AEP are 347 nm and 440 nm (Fig. 1a), respectively. As shown in Fig. 1b, the emission spectrum of CA-AEP shows excitation-independent emission behavior at different excitation wavelengths (315–400 nm), which is similar to other non-aromatic fluorescent materials that have been reported [20,24–26]. Moreover, when the excitation wavelength is in the range of 265–300 nm, the emission wavelengths are also concentrated at 440 nm (Figure S5), but the emission intensity is much weaker than that excited at 315–400 nm because the isolated chromophore consumes too much energy through non-radiative transitions. Fig. 1c shows that the fluorescence lifetime attenuation curve of CA-AEP is a double exponential fit, and the fluorescence lifetime is 6.68 ns, similar to some natural amino acids [11].

The optical properties of CA-AEP were further studied. Figure S6 shows photographs of different concentrations of CA-AEP aqueous solution under a 365 nm UV lamp. It is found that the dilute solution of CA-AEP emits weak light, but its fluorescence intensity increases significantly with increasing concentration. And, under the excitation of 347 nm, the emission spectrum clearly shows that the emission intensity is concentration-dependent, and the wavelengths are concentrated at 440 nm (Figure S7b). And the white CA-AEP solid emits bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 1d). The above data clearly shows that CA-AEP has aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties [24,27–30]. However, it is worth noting that the absorption peak intensity derived from the n-π* electron transition at 347 nm also increases significantly with the increase in concentration (Figure S7a). At the same time, the DLS results show that as the CA-AEP concentration increases from 0.1 mM to 10.0 mM, the particle size also increases from 1.29 nm to 4.80 nm (Figure S8), which can be explained as larger clusters generate stronger spatial electron delocalization system, and ultimately make the absorption peak intensity stronger. We believe that the main reason for the AIE behavior comes from CTE mechanism. Therefore, the space electron delocalization system formed by hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl clusters is considered to be the luminescence center [20]. Of course, in the aggregate state, the weakening of the non-radiative transition caused by the molecular rotation (especially the tertiary amine part) and its vibration is part of the reason for the enhanced fluorescence emission [8]. Finally, the best excitation wavelengths under different emission wavelengths (400–560 nm) are concentrated at 347 nm (Figure S9), which proves that the luminescence in this band only comes from one luminescence center.

The aqueous solution of CA-AEP can emit strong bionic fluorescence by adjusting the pH. The detailed mechanism of this surprising behavior is fully explored. Fig. 2
a is the fluorescence picture of CA-AEP in aqueous solution of different pH (2.0 to 13.0) under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp. It was found that the change in fluorescence was not obvious under acidic conditions, but under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CA-AEP solution increased significantly with the gradual increase of pH, so that a visible effect of turning on fluorescence could be achieved. This property makes it possible to easily determine the acidity and alkalinity of aqueous solutions. The fluorescence emission (440 nm) intensity values of CA-AEP solution at different pH values were recorded in detail (Fig. 2b). Interestingly, it was found that CA-AEP had the lowest fluorescence emission intensity at pH = 7.0, and the fluorescence emission of CA-AEP would increase whether increasing or decreasing the pH of the solution. From the results of zeta potential, the isoelectric point (pI) of CA-AEP is close to 7.0 (Fig. 2c), which may be the cause of the weakest fluorescence at pH = 7.0.

First, the reason for the increase in fluorescence under acidic conditions was analyzed. When the pH value of the solution decreased from 7.0 to 2.0, the fluorescence intensity of CA-AEP increased slowly and linearly (Figure S10). The absolute values of the zeta potential in this process increased significantly (Fig. 2c), indicating that the intramolecular cations are gradually increasing, and the system is becoming more decentralized and stable. According to related literature [2], the increase in fluorescence may be due to the deepening protonation of CA-AEP , which fills the molecule with cations (Scheme 1 b), and the strong charge repulsion makes the structure of the molecule more rigid, thereby limiting the movement of the molecule, which in turn enhances the fluorescence emission. In addition, the TEM results show that well-dispersed aggregates with a particle size of about 5 nm is formed under this condition (Figure S11), which may be due to the self-assembly of the charged molecules during the drying process.

Next, the reasons for the significant enhancement of CA-AEP fluorescence under alkaline conditions were analyzed in detail. After careful observation, we found that this increasing trend is periodic (Fig. 2 d-g). First, when the pH of the solution increased from 7.0 to 10.0, the fluorescence intensity of CA-AEP increased relatively slowly, while the change in absorption wavelength was relatively stable (Fig. 2d and e). In addition, the absolute values of the zeta potential at this time show an increasing trend (Fig. 2c). Therefore, these evidences indicate that the enhancement of fluorescence at this stage may be due to the deprotonation process of CA-AEP molecules under alkaline conditions, which cause the molecules to be negative charged, and the charge repulsion between the molecules inhibits the movement of the molecules, similar to the protonation process.

Thereafter, as the pH of the solution (10.0 to 12.0) continued to increase, the absorption, excitation, and emission peak intensity of CA-AEP increased significantly simultaneously and all showed a clear linear relationship (inset in Fig. 2, R2 are 0.995 and 0.994). In particular, when the pH is 12.0, the absolute QY of CA-AEP increases to 11.2%, which is called CA-AEP 12. The increase in the n-π* electron transition intensity in the absorption spectra indicate that rapid cluster aggregation may be occurring at this time. By examining the SEM results of CA-AEP at different pH, it was found that only a few nanowires were formed when the pH of the solution was 7.0 (Figure S12a), and there was indeed significant cluster aggregation when the pH was 12.0 (Figure S12b). The particle size results show that as the pH of the solution increases from 7.0 to 12.0, the particle size increases from 1.29 nm to 7.82 nm (Figure S13), which was consistent with the results of SEM and absorption spectra. At this time, the result of zeta potential showed that its absolute value began to increase significantly (Fig. 2c). According to the zeta potential results, the stepwise ionization process of CA-AEP was carefully analyzed. It is worth noting that the initial pH value (pH = 10.0) at this stage seems to be close to the pK3 of the secondary amine at the end of CA-AEP (Fig. 3). Therefore, we attribute the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement at this stage to that the deprotonation process of the secondary amine at the end of the CA-AEP molecule enhances the intermolecular interaction (intra / intermolecular hydrogen bonding), which causes the molecules to aggregate into large and distinct clusters (Scheme 1 b), namely CTE mechanism [31,32]. The generation of clusters enhances the space electron delocalization system and limits the rotation and vibration of molecules, thus enhancing fluorescence emission.

When the pH value increased from 12.0 to 13.0, the fluorescence increased slightly, but the absorbance decreased. The particle size results showed that as the pH of the solution increased from 12.0 to 13.0, the particle size distribution of 7.82nm changed to two distributions of 4.18 nm and 17.12 nm (Figure S13). Combined with Fluorescence (440 nm) and absorption (347 nm) intensity measured by repeatedly adjusting the pH of the CA-AEP solution between 13.0 and 12.0 (Figure S14), we speculate that the deprotonation of the tertiary amine (pK4) of the CA-AEP molecule changed its particle size distribution at this stage. Larger clusters are good for luminescence, while smaller clusters reduce overall absorption. A clearer explanation requires us to conduct more in-depth research on it in future work.

It is worth noting that the fluorescence enhancement effect of CA-AEP under extremely alkaline conditions far exceeds the fluorescence enhancement caused by concentration changes. The reason may be that the difference in the tightness of the two cluster structures (Figure S15). CA-AEP molecules gather together when the concentration of solution increases, and the compactness between molecules is poor, resulting in a relatively small spatial electron delocalization system. However, under extremely alkaline conditions, deprotonation process causes strong interaction between molecules to form close clusters, resulting in stronger spatial electron delocalization system.

The sensitive response of CA-AEP to alkaline pH values means that it has very important practical application value. Interesting, when the pH value of the CA-AEP solution was adjusted from 12.0 to 7.4 (called CA-AEP 12-7.4), the fluorescence and absorption intensity remained basically unchanged, and the fluorescence did not decrease significantly after three cycles of pH adjustment (Fig. 4 ). The particle size distribution proves that there is no significant change in particle size before and after pH adjustment. It is speculated that the clusters formed at extremely alkaline pH may be difficult to separate again during pH adjustment. The result is that CA-AEP does not have the potential to dynamically monitor the environmental pH of the system, but can monitor the maximum dynamic pH that the system has ever reached. It is speculated that CA-AEP will play a huge role in monitoring the maximum dynamic pH of various systems, especially biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CA-AEP for cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: CA-AEP molecules emit weak light under biological pH (7.4) conditions, and emit fluorescence sufficient for biological imaging under extremely alkaline conditions. As a luminous biomimetic molecule with a dipeptide structure, it has the potential to be applied in the biological field and helps to understand the autofluorescence of biological systems. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the cytotoxicity of this luminogen and apply it to cell imaging. As described in the experimental section, the standard MTT method was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CA-AEP molecules. The results are shown in Fig. 5 a. When A549 cells were cultured with 50 μM CA-AEP 12-7.4 molecules for 24 h, the cell survival rate was still above 80%, indicating that CA-AEP 12-7.4 has low cytotoxicity. In addition, A549 cells were cultured in the presence of CA-AEP 12-7.4 (50 μM) at 37 °C for 2 h, and then bright fluorescence was observed in the cytoplasm by confocal fluorescence microscope, the shape and morphology of the cells were not obvious after incubation change (Fig. 5b). These results prove its potential for application in the field of cell biology.

In summary, we have described a non-conjugated, nitrogen-containing small-molecule fluorescent probe that does not contain a conventional chromophore, and it was synthesized by the condensation of anhydrous citric acid and N-aminoethylpiperazine. This molecule shows AIE characteristics, and it is substantially non-luminescent in dilute solutions but exhibits bright blue emission when aggregated or in solid state. However, absorption spectrum and DLS tests confirmed that its AIE behavior was derived from the CTE mechanism, which was a spatially delocalized system created by overlapping lone pairs of nitrogen and oxygen electrons in the cluster. Most importantly, the compound is a new type of non-conjugated fluorescent molecule with a dipeptide structure, which can emit strong biomimetic fluorescence in aqueous solutions by adjusting the pH of the solution. It also realizes the monitoring of the maximum pH in the alkaline aqueous solution. Finally, the low toxicity to cells and good cell imaging effects show that CA-AEP has the potential to be applied in the field of cell biology.

In this work, we have tried our best to explain the fluorescence emission mechanism and pH response mechanism of CA-AEP molecules. However, more fluorescent molecules with similar structures should be synthesized, and their relationship between different molecular structures and luminescence behavior will be further studied. In addition, the different cluster states, cluster types, and environmental factors affecting clusters related to fluorescence emission are not very clear, which will be the main research direction in the future.

Yunqian Ma: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Hao Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Kexin Wang: Validation. Duxia Cao: Writing - review & editing. Mingkai Wang: Supervision. Ruifang Guan: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Chuanjian Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21477047, 51903139) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119604.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A unique aliphatic tertiary amine chromophore: fluorescence, polymer structure, and application in cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Sun, C.-Y. Hong, C.-Y. Pan, A Unique Aliphatic Tertiary Amine Chromophore: Fluorescence, Polymer Structure, and Application in Cell Imaging, Journal of the American Chemical Society 134(51) (2012) 20581-20584.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence emission from dendrimers and its pH dependence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, T. Imae, Fluorescence Emission from Dendrimers and Its pH Dependence, Journal of the American Chemical Society 126(41) (2004) 13204-13205.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unprecedented blue intrinsic photoluminescence from hyperbranched and linear polyethylenimines: polymer architectures and pH-effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pastor-Pérez L., Chen Y., Shen Z., Lahoz A., Stiriba S.-E., Unprecedented blue intrinsic photoluminescence from hyperbranched and linear polyethylenimines: polymer architectures and pH-effects, Macromol. Rapid Commun. 28 (13) (2007) 1404 – 1409.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and fluorescent properties of biodegradable hyperbranched poly(amido amine)s

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang W., Pan C.-Y., Synthesis and fluorescent properties of biodegradable hyperbranched poly(amido amine)s, Macromol. Rapid Commun. 30 (24) (2009) 2096 – 2101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent hyperbranched poly(amido amine) containing β-cyclodextrin as a nonviral gene delivery vector

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, L. Zhou, Y. Pang, W. Huang, F. Qiu, X. Jiang, X. Zhu, D. Yan, Q. Chen, Photoluminescent Hyperbranched Poly(amido amine) Containing β-Cyclodextrin as a Nonviral Gene Delivery Vector, Bioconjugate Chemistry 22(6) (2011) 1162-1170.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong fluorescence emission from PEGylated hyperbranched poly(amido amine)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, Z.-Q. Yu, C.-Y. Hong, Y.-Z. You, Strong fluorescence emission from PEGylated hyperbranched poly(amido amine), European Polymer Journal 49(12) (2013) 4189-4194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of hyperbranched poly(ether amide)s with thermoresponsive property and unexpected strong blue photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Lin, J.-W. Gao, H.-W. Liu, Y.-S. Li, Synthesis and Characterization of Hyperbranched Poly(ether amide)s with Thermoresponsive Property and Unexpected Strong Blue Photoluminescence, Macromolecules 42(9) (2009) 3237-3246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiple stimuli-responsive fluorescent sensor from citric acid and 1-(2-aminoethyl)piperazine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Yang, F. Liu, R. Li, X. Wang, W. Hao, Multiple Stimuli-Responsive Fluorescent Sensor from Citric Acid and 1-(2-Aminoethyl)piperazine, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 10(10) (2018) 9123-9128.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prevalent intrinsic emission from nonaromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, W. Luo, H. Ma, Q. Peng, W.Z. Yuan, Y. Zhang, Prevalent intrinsic emission from nonaromatic amino acids and poly(amino acids), Science China Chemistry 61(3) (2018) 351-359.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near UV-Visible electronic absorption originating from charged amino acids in a monomeric protein

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Prasad, I. Mandal, S. Singh, A. Paul, B. Mandal, R. Venkatramani, R. Swaminathan, Near UV-Visible electronic absorption originating from charged amino acids in a monomeric protein, Chemical Science 8(8) (2017) 5416-5433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural chemistry enables fluorescence of amino acids in the crystalline solid state

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Ravanfar, C.J. Bayles, A. Abbaspourrad, Structural Chemistry Enables Fluorescence of Amino Acids in the Crystalline Solid State, Crystal Growth & Design 20(3) (2020) 1673-1680.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solution-state characteristics of the ultraviolet A-induced visible fluorescence from proteins

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Guptasarma, Solution-state characteristics of the ultraviolet A-induced visible fluorescence from proteins, Archives of Biochemistry and Biophysics 478(2) (2008) 127-129.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel absorption and fluorescence characteristics of L-lysine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Homchaudhuri L. , Swaminathan R. , Novel absorption and fluorescence characteristics of L-lysine , Chem. Lett. 30 ( 8 ) (2001) 844 – 845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clustering-triggered emission and persistent room temperature phosphorescence of sodium alginate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Dou, Q. Zhou, X. Chen, Y. Tan, X. He, P. Lu, K. Sui, B.Z. Tang, Y. Zhang, W.Z. Yuan, Clustering-Triggered Emission and Persistent Room Temperature Phosphorescence of Sodium Alginate, Biomacromolecules 19(6) (2018) 2014-2022.
